serving.description: 1 bowl (3 rice balls in soy bean milk)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one bowl containing three black sesame filled glutinous rice balls served in chilled soy bean milk, weighing approximately 300 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Soy Bean Milk and Glutinous Rice Ball (Tang Yuan) component data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for this combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated by blending HPB Singapore Foodo data for (1) glutinous rice balls with black sesame filling and (2) sweetened soy bean milk, weighted by approximate component proportions in a 300 g serving (approx. 150 g rice balls + 150 g soy bean milk). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this blended estimate and have been retained with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for glutinous rice (USDA #20052) and soymilk (USDA #16222). Energy 106.7 kcal/100g, protein 3.5 g, fat 4.0 g, carbohydrate 14.0 g are plausible for this dish composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database reference for glutinous rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific combined dish (black sesame tang yuan in soy bean milk). GI estimated at 86 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: glutinous rice flour, which has a published GI of approximately 86–98 in the University of Sydney GI Database (glutinous rice, boiled; GI ~86, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The soy bean milk component has a low GI (~34) but constitutes approximately 50% of serving weight and a minority of total carbohydrate; the blended dish GI is dominated by the glutinous rice flour. A conservative blended estimate of GI 86 is used. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (86 × 42.0) / 100 = 36.1, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 14.0 × 3 = 42.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (8.33 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g); sodium is low at 13.3 mg/100 g but added sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit this dessert to occasional consumption and request unsweetened soy bean milk to reduce added sugar intake, as dietary sugar at this level with low fibre independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~86) driven by glutinous rice flour (GI ~86–98), combined with 8.33 g sugar/100 g and only 1.0 g dietary fibre/100 g, producing a glycemic load of 36.1 per standard 300 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dessert is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high glycemic index and glycemic load; if consumed, portion should be strictly limited to one rice ball with unsweetened soy bean milk and paired with a low-GI meal to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 0.83 g/100 g and sodium is low at 13.3 mg/100 g; however, added sugar at 8.33 g/100 g with low fibre (1.0 g/100 g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and triglyceride elevation risk per AHA dietary guidelines
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional dessert only; the low saturated fat and sodium profile is favourable, but the high sugar and low fibre content can adversely affect triglyceride levels and should be moderated in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined high-GI glutinous rice flour and added sugar (8.33 g/100 g) with low fibre promote pro-inflammatory pathways; black sesame provides modest antioxidants but insufficient to offset the refined carbohydrate base
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit this dessert as the high-GI glutinous rice and added sugar may promote systemic inflammation relevant to disease flares; substituting with a lower-GI grain-based dessert is preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Sesame Glutinous Rice Ball Tang Yuan in Soy Bean Milk (3 pieces, chilled) is a traditional Chinese dessert popular in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. The dish consists of three boiled glutinous rice balls (tang yuan / 汤圆) filled with a smooth, sweetened black sesame paste, served chilled in a bowl of sweetened soy bean milk. The glutinous rice skin is soft, chewy, and slightly translucent, with a mild neutral flavour that contrasts with the rich, nutty, and slightly sweet black sesame filling. The soy bean milk base is smooth, lightly sweet, and refreshing when served cold. This dessert is traditionally associated with the Lantern Festival (元宵节) and Winter Solstice (冬至) celebrations, though it is widely available year-round in Singapore hawker centres, dessert stalls, and food delivery platforms. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 320 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 12.0 g fat, and 42.0 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is high (~86) due to the glutinous rice flour base, making this dessert unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The dish is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free, and contains soy as the primary allergen. Calcium from soy bean milk contributes approximately 100 mg per serving. The dessert is low in sodium (40 mg per serving) but high in added sugar (~25 g per serving), which is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this exact combined dish (black sesame tang yuan served in chilled soy bean milk). Nutrient values are estimated by blending HPB Singapore Foodo component data for glutinous rice balls and soy bean milk, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. The input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) is consistent with this estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant glutinous rice flour component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this combined dish (glutinous rice ball + soy bean milk) is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific combined dish preparation are not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this plant-based dessert with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with HPB and USDA data for similar preparations. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a fully plant-based dish (glutinous rice flour, black sesame, soy bean milk) with no animal-derived ingredients; consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy soymilk (USDA #16222) and glutinous rice (USDA #20052) entries. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this dessert; no vitamin C-containing ingredients are present. 6. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice flour (dominant carbohydrate source); the soy bean milk component has a low GI (~34) but contributes a minority of total carbohydrate. 7. Nutrient source is an estimated blend; a direct HPB entry for this exact dish was not located as of the access date.
